spec_match.rationale: The page advertises @container anchored(--name) but the script does its own JS-level fits-in-bounds calculation, picks a fallback name, and applies an `.f-bottom`/`.f-top`/etc. class. None of the new container-query syntax is being exercised — the demo would render identically on Chrome 142 because the CSS doesn't depend on the v143 feature.

interactive.rationale: Two range sliders (X/Y position) drive a live popover restyle with active fallback name and synthesised query string echoed in the panel. Real interactivity.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb claims 'descendants restyle via @container anchored(--name)'. The page restyles, but not through that mechanism — the JS computes the fallback name and applies a className that conventional CSS selectors then match against.

edge_cases.rationale: Walks through four fallbacks (top/bottom/left/right) and falls back to 'bottom' if nothing fits. Doesn't demonstrate the @container anchored(fallback: flip-block) syntax explicitly named in the chromestatus example, nor the 'no fallback chosen' case where position-try-options is exhausted.

design_system.rationale: Variables-only colour palette; no raw hex captured.

openQuestions.title: Replace the JS fits-in-bounds logic with real anchor positioning
openQuestions.detail: The whole point of this concept is to teach @container anchored(--name). Drop the JS that computes which fallback fits and instead use `position-try-options: --top, --right, --left, --bottom` on the popover with `container-type: anchored`. Then add `@container anchored(--top) { .popover-sim { background: var(--accent-blue); } }` etc. so the colour changes are actually driven by the container query.

openQuestions.title: Add the spec's `fallback: flip-block` example verbatim
openQuestions.detail: ChromeStatus shows `@container anchored(fallback: flip-block)` as the canonical example. Add a small worked snippet on the page that pastes this exact syntax and renders it live so visitors associate the new syntax with the spec.

openQuestions.title: Show the @container anchored() CSS that mirrors the JS
openQuestions.detail: Even if the JS path stays for fallback, surface the equivalent CSS (`@container anchored(--bottom) { ... }`) in a code panel next to the live preview, with the active rule highlighted. Right now the page tells visitors anchored() is being used but they cannot see the CSS.

openQuestions.title: Add a CSS.supports probe for `container-type: anchored`
openQuestions.detail: Older browsers will see the JS simulation and never know they're missing the feature. A small probe panel at the top of the page would tell them whether real anchored container queries are available.

summary: Headline 'use the new @container anchored() query' demo that doesn't actually use it — the script does the work that the CSS feature was designed to do. This is the most important critique of the four concepts in this feature: rewrite the page to be CSS-driven with JS only as a fallback.
